result:
The hum is always there, beneath everything. Not a sound, but a frequency. The vibration of the refrigerator, the latent buzz in the wires inside the walls, the silent, insistent spin of a dozen hard drives in the world around you. We build our lives on this electric thrum and then learn to tune it out, to call that tuned-out state “quiet.” But it’s never quiet. Not really.

I think that’s why I notice the absences more. The sudden, gut-dropping silence when the power cuts out and that hum vanishes, leaving a void so profound your ears ring with the shock of it. The pause between a question and an answer, when entire futures are born and die. The space on a page where a word has been scribbled out—the ghost of the intention lingering, more powerful sometimes than the chosen word that replaced it.

We are taught to fill the spaces. To speak in the awkward gap, to plug our devices in before the battery icon even turns red, to answer emails immediately, to have an opinion, a playlist, a plan. Silence and emptiness have become conditions to be remedied, like a cavity. But what grows in those cavities? Sometimes, only decay. But sometimes, a pearl.

I remember being small and lying on the scratchy wool carpet in my grandmother’s living room. The clock ticked. That was the only sound, a monumental, wooden tick-tock from a giant thing on the mantelpiece. In that space between ticks, I felt the entire room holding its breath. The dust motes hung in a sunbeam, suspended in the *tock* and waiting for the *tick*. In that suspension, I existed completely. I wasn’t thinking about what came before or next. I was just a thing in a sunbeam, between ticks. It was the first time I felt peace, though I had no word for it. I just knew the space felt full, not empty.

We’ve since traded that kind of space for a different density. The digital space. It’s never empty. It’s a plenum, a howling wilderness of connection and signal. You can shout into it at any hour and hear an echo, often from a stranger on the other side of the planet. It is miraculous. And it is exhausting. Because it leaves no room for the breath-holding. No gap between ticks for the dust to settle and show you what it’s made of.

Perhaps the most radical act now is to cultivate emptiness. Not the emptiness of lack, but the emptiness of a clean bowl, ready to receive. The emptiness of a morning before the phone lights up. The emptiness of a walk without a podcast piping someone else’s thoughts into your skull. It’s in that cultivated emptiness that your own thoughts finally stretch, yawn, and show their shape. They’ve been there all along, huddled under the constant feed, waiting for the feed to stop.

There’s a tree at the edge of the old park. Half of it was struck by lightning years ago. The scar is massive, blackened, a running wound down its trunk. The other half, untouched, is lush and full, heavy with leaves in the summer. People call it “the tragic tree.” But I don’t see tragedy. I see a map. The empty space, the ripped-away half, defines the remaining half utterly. It gives it its story, its character, its stubborn insistence on living. The emptiness isn’t a lack of tree. It is an essential part of the tree’s shape. Without it, it would just be another oak. Now, it is a testament.

We are all like that, I think. Our silences, our losses, the things we didn’t say, the paths we didn’t take—they are not just voids where life isn’t. They are the negative spaces that sculpt the positive ones. The job you didn’t get shapes the career you have. The goodbye you couldn’t say sharpens the memory of the hello. We spend so much energy lamenting the empty space, trying to fill it with noise or distraction or lesser things, that we fail to see how its very presence is holding up the outline of who we are.

So I am trying to learn the grammar of absence. To read the pauses. To listen to the hum not as a nuisance, but as the baseline note of modern life, the pedal tone. To sometimes pull the plug and sit in the ringing, real silence that follows. To leave the page blank for a while, to let the cursor blink like a patient lighthouse, before the next word arrives.

Because the next word always arrives. The light always comes back on. The hum returns. The gap ends. But if you’ve paid attention to the gap, you come back different. You carry a bit of that stillness with you into the noise. You become a kind of walking negative space yourself, a quiet edit in the noisy text of the world.

And in a world shouting to fill every second, every pixel, every frequency, sometimes the most meaningful thing you can offer is a well-placed silence. A breath held. A space between ticks where the dust can dance, and someone else—maybe a child on a carpet—can look up and see, for a fleeting moment, the shape of the peace that holds everything together. Not in spite of the empty spaces, but because of them.

The hum is always there. But so is the silence underneath it. I am learning to listen for both.
